---
title: Managing State
description: Store, retrieve, and manage consistent state in Virtual Objects
---

Virtual Objects provide built-in key-value storage with strong consistency guarantees. State is automatically persisted when functions complete and restored when they restart.

## Get state

Getting state is like looking up a value in a dictionary using a key. If the key exists, you get the stored value. If it doesn't exist, you get `null` (or your default value).

The state is loaded from durable storage when your function starts, so `get()` calls are fast - they read from memory, not from disk or network.

```typescript TypeScript
export const getProfile = async (ctx: ObjectContext) => {
  const profile = await ctx.get<UserProfile>("profile");
  const name = (await ctx.get<string>("name")) ?? "Unknown User";
  
  return {
    profile,
    name,
    hasProfile: profile !== null
  };
};
```

```java Java
@Handler
public ProfileResponse getProfile(ObjectContext ctx) {
    UserProfile profile = ctx.get("profile", UserProfile.class);
    String name = ctx.get("name", String.class).orElse("Unknown User");
    
    return new ProfileResponse(profile, name, profile != null);
}
```

```python Python
async def get_profile(ctx: ObjectContext):
    profile = await ctx.get("profile", UserProfile)
    name = await ctx.get("name", str) or "Unknown User"
    
    return {
        "profile": profile,
        "name": name,
        "has_profile": profile is not None
    }
```

State keys are strings (like `"profile"` or `"lastLogin"`). Values can be any JSON-serializable data. If a key doesn't exist, you get `null` unless you provide a default.

## Set state

Setting state stores a value that will persist even after your function finishes. The next time any function for this Virtual Object runs, it can retrieve this value using the same key.

State changes are automatically saved when your function completes successfully. If your function fails partway through, state changes are rolled back - this keeps your data consistent.

```typescript TypeScript
export const updateProfile = async (ctx: ObjectContext, profile: UserProfile) => {
  // Set multiple pieces of state atomically
  ctx.set("profile", profile);
  ctx.set("lastUpdated", new Date().toISOString());
  ctx.set("version", ((await ctx.get<number>("version")) ?? 0) + 1);
  
  return { 
    success: true, 
    profile,
    updatedAt: new Date().toISOString() 
  };
};
```

```java Java
@Handler
public UpdateResult updateProfile(ObjectContext ctx, UserProfile profile) {
    // Set multiple pieces of state atomically
    ctx.set("profile", profile);
    ctx.set("lastUpdated", Instant.now().toString());
    
    Integer version = ctx.get("version", Integer.class).orElse(0);
    ctx.set("version", version + 1);
    
    return new UpdateResult(true, profile, Instant.now().toString());
}
```

```python Python
async def update_profile(ctx: ObjectContext, profile: UserProfile):
    # Set multiple pieces of state atomically
    await ctx.set("profile", profile)
    await ctx.set("lastUpdated", datetime.now().isoformat())
    
    version = await ctx.get("version", int) or 0
    await ctx.set("version", version + 1)
    
    return {
        "success": True,
        "profile": profile,
        "updated_at": datetime.now().isoformat()
    }
```

All state changes in a function are applied atomically - either all changes are saved when the function completes, or none are if it fails.

## Clear state

Clearing state removes a key-value pair completely. After clearing, attempts to get that key will return `null` (or your default value).

```typescript TypeScript
export const deleteProfile = async (ctx: ObjectContext) => {
  // Get current profile before deletion for audit log
  const profile = await ctx.get<UserProfile>("profile");
  
  if (!profile) {
    return { success: false, error: "Profile not found" };
  }
  
  // Clear all user data atomically
  ctx.clear("profile");
  ctx.clear("tempData");
  ctx.clear("preferences");
  
  // Record deletion timestamp
  ctx.set("deletedAt", new Date().toISOString());
  
  return { success: true, deletedProfile: profile };
};
```

```java Java
@Handler
public DeleteResult deleteProfile(ObjectContext ctx) {
    // Get current profile before deletion for audit log
    UserProfile profile = ctx.get("profile", UserProfile.class);
    
    if (profile == null) {
        return new DeleteResult(false, "Profile not found", null);
    }
    
    // Clear all user data atomically
    ctx.clear("profile");
    ctx.clear("tempData");
    ctx.clear("preferences");
    
    // Record deletion timestamp
    ctx.set("deletedAt", Instant.now().toString());
    
    return new DeleteResult(true, null, profile);
}
```

Clearing completely removes the key, saving storage space and making it clearer in your code that you don't want this data anymore.

## State consistency guarantees

Restate provides strong consistency guarantees that make state management reliable:

**Single-writer guarantee**: Only one function can modify a Virtual Object's state at any given time. This prevents race conditions where two functions try to update the same data simultaneously.

**Atomic commits**: All state changes in a function are applied together when the function completes successfully. If the function fails at any point, all state changes are rolled back.

**Isolation**: Each Virtual Object's state is completely separate from every other object's state. Even if you have a million user accounts, each user's state is isolated from all others.

**Durability**: Once a function completes and state is saved, that data will survive service crashes, restarts, network failures, or even complete data center outages.

## Common patterns

### Counter pattern
Perfect for tracking counts, likes, or any numeric value:

```typescript
let count = (await ctx.get<number>("count")) ?? 0;
count++;
ctx.set("count", count);
return count;
```

The `?? 0` provides a default value for the first time this runs. The single-writer guarantee ensures the count is always accurate.

### Shopping cart pattern  
Manages collections of items that change over time:

```typescript
const items = (await ctx.get<CartItem[]>("items")) ?? [];
items.push(newItem);
ctx.set("items", items);
ctx.set("lastUpdated", Date.now());
```

Both the items and timestamp are updated together atomically.

### User session pattern
Validates and manages time-sensitive data:

```typescript
const session = await ctx.get<Session>("session");
if (!session || session.expires < Date.now()) {
  ctx.clear("session");
  throw new Error("Session expired");
}
return session;
```

The session is validated every time it's accessed and cleaned up if expired.
